lines.text: Ridgemont Middle School, second week of the year. The science room still smells like floor wax and somebody's burnt lunch experiment from last period.
Trimester project. One model, one scientific principle, presented to a real district judge in one week.
And no — before anyone asks — a volcano is not a scientific principle. It's a craft project with a grudge.
Please don't be alone. Please don't be alone.
Groups are on the board. Find your name, find your people, read the rubric before you touch a glue gun.
Dez Okafor. Everyone knows Dez Okafor. And... Priya. Lunchroom girl. Cool. Cool. Love it here already.
Castillo, right? Cool, cool. I already got ideas — we're gonna be fine. We're gonna be great, actually.
We haven't even sat down yet.
That's the confidence talking. Ride the wave, Castillo.
Hi.
lines.action: small wave, doesn't quite make eye contact

lines.text: Hey. I'm Wren.
I know. Attendance.
Okay. That was a joke. That was almost funny. Noted.
One week, people. Clock's already running.
Okay so — don't hate me yet.
lines.action: pulls a flash drive out of his backpack

lines.text: My brother did basically this exact project. Two years ago. Got an A.
Basically this exact project, or...?
Same principle. Same setup, pretty much. We update the numbers a little, make it ours. Nobody's gonna check a seventh-grade science fair against a two-year-old flash drive.
And there it is. The look. The 'please somebody else say something' look.
It's not cheating, it's efficiency. There's a difference — look it up.
Priya says nothing. All eyes, without meaning to, land on Wren.
...So? We doing this the fast way or the slow way?
Fine, we'll try it the real way first.
Okay. Principle. Go.
Something we can actually build in a week without setting off a fire alarm.
...We could do density. Layers, different liquids. It's simple, but it looks cool if you do it right.
lines.action: taps pen once, barely looks up

lines.text: ...huh. Okay. That's actually not bad.
I mean, it's just an idea.
No — that's good. That's actually good.

lines.action: brief eye contact, then looks away — the ghost of a smile

lines.text: Okay. Maybe this works.
See — look, the whole structure's already here. Hypothesis, method, even the graph template.
That is... a lot of a project already done.
Right? My brother's actually a genius, he just doesn't use it for anything useful.

lines.action: stares at the screen, pen taps once, then stops

lines.text: She's not saying anything. That probably means something.
Priya, you good?
It's fine.
lines.emotion: the tone that means it isn't

lines.text: This is gonna save us so much time. We can actually make the model look good instead of scrambling last minute.
It is a lot of time saved. That's... not nothing.
I mean — it's just an idea, but... what if we simplified it? Cut the extra trial, make the display more visual. It'd actually be more interesting to watch.
That'll take too long. We're doing it my way.
There it is again. Same thing as the lunchroom, day one. Except now I actually know her name.
We're already halfway there. We don't need to redesign the whole thing four days out.
...it's probably not a big deal.
It's kind of a big deal, though.
So we good? Moving on.
Actually — I think Priya's idea's better. It's simpler, and it'll actually hold up when we're presenting live.
...Okay, whoa. When did this become two-on-one?
It's not. I just think she's right.
...Fine. Fine! We'll look at it. Don't gang up on me, that's all I'm asking.

lines.action: sits up straighter than Wren's seen her sit all week

lines.text: ...Thanks.
Wasn't anything.
It was something.
Great. Moving on.

lines.action: pen goes still on the sketchbook — not tapping, just still

lines.text: I didn't say anything. I know I didn't say anything.
Priya, can you pass the—
Yeah.
lines.action: hands it over, doesn't look up

lines.text: That's not shy. I've seen shy. This is different.
Priya doesn't say anything else for the rest of the session. Nobody else seems to notice.
Hey — Priya, wait up.
Back in there, with the trial idea — that was a good call. Genuinely, the simpler version's better.
I mean — it's probably not a big deal.
It's a big deal. I just should've said that in there instead of out here.
...Yeah. Kind of.
lines.action: brief sharp eye contact, then away

lines.text: But thanks. For saying it at all, I guess.
Next time I'll say it where it counts.
Okay, so trial three's just... gone. The numbers don't make sense and we don't have time to redo it.
How not-make-sense are we talking?
Enough that if Ms. Alvarez looks at it for more than five seconds she's gonna ask questions.
But — hear me out. We know what the result's supposed to look like. Every other trial points the same direction. We just... smooth it out. It's basically the same thing, just cleaner.
...That's not the same thing.
It's basically the same thing.
It's not, though.
The clock on the kitchen wall is suddenly the loudest thing in the room.
Guys, it's midnight math. We're not committing fraud, we're rounding up a little.
Okay, camera on the beaker — Priya, can you see the—
Tilt it left. No — your left.
This is my left!
It's really not.
He's very confident about his lefts.
I contain multitudes, Castillo.
Wait — go back. Your ratio's off. You added the second measurement before the first one settled.
Priya, that's it — that's actually the fix.
I know.
Okay, she knows. She's not gonna let us forget it either.
Okay, so if we just bump trial three up to match the trend—
...Yeah. Just — do it.
See, look, that's clean. That looks like real data now.
It's fine.
Okay. We're set. G'night.
Night.
Priya doesn't say anything. She just leaves the call. The 'left the call' notification sits there a second too long.
Nobody said goodnight the normal way. I don't know why I noticed that.
The judge sits in the back row with a clipboard. Ms. Alvarez walks the room, pen tapping twice against her palm.
—and as you can see, all four trials support the hypothesis pretty conclusively.
These results are unusually clean.
Walk me through how you got this measurement.
Silence. Three sets of eyes land on Wren without meaning to.
That one's just — solid data. Good trial.
Wren?
lines.action: still looking at her, not raising her voice

lines.text: I'm not thrilled. I'm also not going to pretend I didn't hear you say that.
You'll present with a note about the data — the judge deserves to know what she's actually looking at.
...Yeah, okay. That wasn't — I shouldn't have pushed that. Whatever. It's not a big deal.
...It kind of is, though.
...Thanks.
Go on. Present what you've got. That's still a real project under there.
Priya, is that right?
lines.action: studies Priya a beat too long

lines.text: ...I guess.
Alright.

lines.emotion: relief plain on his face, doesn't look at Priya

lines.text: The presentation continues. Priya doesn't say another word for the rest of the period.
She didn't even look at me. I don't blame her.
It was kind of a group effort — timeline got tight for all of us, and some of the later numbers got smoothed over more than they should've.
Walk me through it. Whose numbers, specifically?
...It was kind of all of us, honestly.
Alright.
lines.action: moves on to the next group, caveat noted

lines.text: Nobody's humiliated. Nobody's fully honest either. I feel that gap the rest of the period.
...My mom's usually late. So. We've got a minute.
Cool. Cool.
Today was a lot.
Yeah.
A few yards off, Dez is still fighting with his bike lock, muttering at it under his breath.
Last chance. Say something now, or don't say it at all.
Hey — about today. I should've said something back there, before Ms. Alvarez had to ask. I'm sorry I let it get that far.
...You don't have to—
I know. I want to anyway.
New rule. Official. No faking results, no talking over each other — I'll write it in the margin so it's binding.
You'd really write that down.
I have a whole system. Don't test me.
...Okay. Deal.
lines.emotion: small, but she believes it this time

lines.text: So, uh — you doing anything this weekend?
Not really. Probably just... nothing.
Nothing's underrated.
Yeah.
lines.emotion: small smile, doesn't reach her eyes

lines.text: That's her.
lines.action: quick wave, climbs into the car

lines.text: Easier this way. Also lonelier than I thought it'd be.
Hey. Can I say something?
If it's about the lock, I already know it's broken.
It's not about the lock.
...Okay.
Today — and honestly the whole project — you kept cutting Priya off. And when it went wrong, you let it land on her instead of you. That wasn't okay.
Whoa — I didn't make you say anything back there. That was all you.
I know. I'm not saying it wasn't. I'm saying you knew it wasn't her fault, and you didn't say anything either.
...Yeah, okay. That wasn't — I shouldn't have talked to her like that. Whatever. It's not a big deal.
...It's kind of a big deal.

options.text: That's basically cheating. Let's do our own.
I mean... it could save us time. Let's just look.

a.text: Whoa, okay, didn't say steal a car, Castillo.
...Fine. Fine! We'll do it the boring way first, see how far that gets us.

b.text: See, this is why I like you. Efficient.

b.action: taps pen once, looks down at her sketchbook, says nothing

options.text: Back Priya's idea out loud, right now
Stay quiet and let Dez keep leading
Say nothing now, but check in with Priya after

a.text: Actually — I think Priya's idea's better.
...Okay, whoa, when did this become two-on-one?

c.text: I'll say something. Just — not in front of him.

options.text: "Let's just redo it right, even if it takes longer."
"Fine — let's just clean it up and submit."

a.text: I know what time it is.
...Fine. Fine! Grab your stuff. We're doing trial three again.

b.text: See, this is why we're gonna win this.

b.action: goes quiet on her end of the call, doesn't argue again

b.text: Nobody said anything else. That's the part that bothers me.

options.text: Admit the timeline was tight and be honest about what happened
Imply it was Priya's part of the work that went wrong
Give a vague, half-true answer that protects everyone a little

a.text: Trial three didn't come out right the first time. We redid parts of it late, and some of the numbers got adjusted to match what we expected instead of just being what they were.
Okay. Thank you for telling me that in front of everyone instead of after.

b.text: I think that was Priya's section — maybe something happened with her measurements.
Priya's face falls, in front of the whole room, in front of the judge.

c.text: It was kind of a group effort. The timeline got tight for all of us, and I think some of the later numbers got smoothed over more than they should've.
Walk me through it — whose numbers, specifically?

options.text: Apologize to Priya and suggest real ground rules going forward
Let it go and talk about something else
Wait for Priya to leave, then tell Dez directly the pressure wasn't okay

a.text: Hey — about today. I should've said something sooner.

b.text: So, uh — you doing anything this weekend?

c.text: Priya's mom pulls up. A quick wave, and she's gone.

c.action: walks over to Dez at the bike racks

lines.text: Every hard call this week, I picked the slow way. Turns out the slow way and the right way were the same road the whole time.
You know, for someone who talks fast when she's nervous, you don't talk fast when it actually matters.
...Yeah. I noticed that too, actually.
We cut every corner there was to cut this week. Feels strange that the apology's the one thing I didn't shortcut.
...A little late for ground rules, don't you think?
Yeah. I know. I'd still rather say it late than not say it.
...Okay. Late's still something.
Twice now. In the room, and out here. Maybe that's just what I do now.
You keep doing that. Saying the thing.
Somebody's gotta.
Yeah. Glad it's you.
I didn't get everything right this week. But I got this part right, at least at the end.
...Better late than never, I guess.
Yeah. Working on the 'never late' part.
Aren't we all.
I told the truth every time it mattered on paper. Just not the one time it mattered to a person.
See you Monday.
lines.action: the car door closes before Wren can say anything else

lines.text: ...Yeah. See you Monday.
We cut corners all week. Feels like I just cut one more, right at the end.
...Bye, Wren.
She didn't say 'see you Monday.' I noticed that too.
I didn't say anything back there. I'm not saying anything out here either. There's a pattern, and I'm starting to see it.
...See you Monday, I guess.
I guess. Yeah.
Some days I say the thing. Today wasn't one of them.
...See you Monday.
lines.action: small wave, gone before Wren can decide if she means it

lines.text: You're really doing this whole speech thing, huh.
I did the honest thing all week. Feels weird to stop now.
...Yeah. Okay. Noted.
Bit rich, coming from you. We both cut corners this week.
Yeah. We did. That's not what I'm talking about right now.
...Fair. Still fair.
You did this back in the study room too. The whole 'actually, Priya's right' thing.
Yeah. And I'm doing it again.
...Consistent. I'll give you that.
Didn't expect this from you, honestly.
Yeah, well. Some weeks you figure stuff out later than you'd like.
...Tell me about it.

voiceNotes: Wren: fast and deflective under pressure, slows and gets plainer when it actually matters (see her shift from dry one-liners into flat, direct sentences in choice-4a and 11a — no jokes when it counts). Her INTERNAL lines do the emotional labor her spoken lines avoid; she rarely says 'I feel' out loud. Dez: talks in outcomes and sports/efficiency logic even when rationalizing something shaky ('efficiency,' 'rounding up a little'); his real feelings only surface in short, quiet fragments after he's been called out, never as a clean apology. Priya: heavily hedges ('I mean, it's probably not a big deal') until she's earned trust, then drops the hedge entirely — track this shift precisely across the branches: in advocate/integrity paths she reaches unhedged lines (11a, 7a) faster than in avoidant/compliance paths where she stays guarded or goes fully silent. Ms. Alvarez never says 'you're lying' — always 'walk me through it' or silence-plus-a-look, per her profile; her disappointment reads as stillness, not volume.

alternatives: Priya Nassar: 'You say that like it's a personality trait.'
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Priya's dry humor but offers a slightly warmer or slightly sharper flavor depending on how prickly the studio wants her first impression to land.

alternatives: Dez Okafor: 'We're not lying, we're editing.'
Dez Okafor: 'Nobody fails a science fair over decimal points, Wren.'
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alternatives lean harder into Dez's rationalizing-via-reframe pattern if the studio wants the temptation to feel even more 'reasonable-sounding' per the scene's emotional beat.

alternatives: Dez Okafor: '...I'm not gonna say sorry. But I heard you.'
alternativeLines.reasoning: The profile specifies Dez's non-apologies are real but incomplete — these alternates keep that same 'not an apology, not nothing' texture with slightly different bluntness levels.
